coredata.dc:title: Intrinsic negative TCR of superblack carbon aerogel films and their ultrabroad band response from UV to microwave
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Xiujie
Wang, Hongqiang
Chen, Tianze
Zhang, Ting
Chu, Junjun
Du, Ai
coredata.dc:description: Hot-electron effect in the superblack carbon aerogel (CA) may lead to the relatively strong response to long-wavelength electromagnetic wave by comparison with the ohmic loss effect. In this article, we find a negative temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR) with a value of about 0.13–0.15%/K in superblack CA films. Interestingly, the negative TCR is the intrinsic nature of the amorphous CA films, which mainly attributes to the increase of carrier concentration according to the Hall Effect results. The behavior is just similar to a combination of graphite and carbon quantum dots. At last, the CA film with a thickness of about 400 nm was used and exhibited ultrabroad band response from UV light, visible light, infrared ray to microwave, owing to the strong absorption and the high-efficiency photo-thermal conversion induced by hot electrons. The intrinsic properties of superblack CA films may provide a novel route to low-cost and high-efficiency longwave detection.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physical properties measurement of CA films
Photoelectric response test of CA films

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fabricating process of superblack CA film. (b) SEM image of CA film. The inset is the cross profile of the film. (c) TEM image of the powder scraped from CA film. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) C 1s XPS spectra of the CA films carbonized at 800 °C and 1000 °C, respectively. (b) Raman spectra of CA films carbonized at 800 °C and 1000 °C, respectively. (c) TEM image and C and O mapping of the CA films carbonized at 1000 °C. (d) High-resolution TEM image of the CA films carbonized at 1000 °C. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Diffuse reflectivity of the CA films after 800 °C and 1000 °C carbonization, respectively. (b, c) Normalized resistance changes (ΔR/R0 where R0 is the resistance of the films under ambient temperature 293K) and temperature changes (ΔT = T-T0, where T0 = 293K) over laser power of the CA films with carbonization temperature of 800 (b) and 1000 °C (c). (d) ΔR/R0 as a function of temperature under illumination of lasers with different wavelengths with carbonization temperature of 800 °C. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Time response of the CA film under 100 mW irradiation of 532 nm laser and the zoomed-in part of ΔR/R0 with time under irradiation with the fitting curve. (b) Temperature change under irradiation of the hottest spot at the center (Sp1) and the spot at the edge of the film (Sp2). (c) Thermal images of irradiated CA film and the corresponding Sp1 and Sp2 on the film at different time. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The resistivity, (b) carrier concentration, (c) carrier mobility of the CA film after 1000 °C carbonization as a function of temperature. (d) Scheme of the energy band structures of CA film. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Response of the CA film towards electromagnetic waves of different spectrums: (a) Response to the temperature of finger. (b) Response to 300–2500 nm from Xe lamp; (c) Response to far infrared irradiated by 80 °C hot water, with a central wavelength at about 8200 nm calculated by Wien displacement law; (d) Response to 3 cm microwave from a microwave generator. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Intrinsic negative TCR of superblack carbon aerogel films and their ultrabroad band response from UV to microwave

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Special Artificial Microstructure and Technology, School of Physics Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai, 200092, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Special Artificial Microstructure and Technology
School of Physics Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Special Artificial Microstructure and Technology, School of Physics Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai, 200092, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of High Energy Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of High Energy Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hot-electron effect in the superblack carbon aerogel (CA) may lead to the relatively strong response to long-wavelength electromagnetic wave by comparison with the ohmic loss effect. In this article, we find a negative temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR) with a value of about 0.13–0.15%/K in superblack CA films. Interestingly, the negative TCR is the intrinsic nature of the amorphous CA films, which mainly attributes to the increase of carrier concentration according to the Hall Effect results. The behavior is just similar to a combination of graphite and carbon quantum dots. At last, the CA film with a thickness of about 400 nm was used and exhibited ultrabroad band response from UV light, visible light, infrared ray to microwave, owing to the strong absorption and the high-efficiency photo-thermal conversion induced by hot electrons. The intrinsic properties of superblack CA films may provide a novel route to low-cost and high-efficiency longwave detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Superblack materials refer to the materials that can absorb most incident light of a certain band without reflection or transmission. Due to their high absorption, superblack materials play important roles in light absorption [1–3], photo-thermal conversion [4], solar energy utilization [5,6] and other fields. Generally, the electronic and mesoscopic structure will both influence the interaction between light and matter mainly due to the ohmic loss [7–12]. In 2016, we found that the subwavelength structure including primary skeletons and micropores of the carbon aerogels (CAs) strongly affect their reflection. By elaborately constructing the combining microstructure of high porosity, small primary skeleton and high proportion of micropore, an ultra-low total diffuse reflectivity of 0.19% was achieved, which attracted attentions as a superblack material [13]. This study proposed an electromagnetic wave-electron-microstructure indirect interaction mechanism. Due to the small size confinement effect caused by the microporous structure less than 2 nm, the enhanced electron interaction significantly increased the equivalent number density and reduced the mean free path of electrons, resulting in the extremely weak reflection. Interestingly, the microstructure seems not to affect the electron-phonon interaction since the electrical conductivities of CAs are only determined by their density. This indicates that the absorbed energy in superblack CAs is not only directly converted into joule heat through electron-phonon coupling, but also stored in the electrons, making them hot. Therefore, the energy of hot electrons may be applied to direct light conversion or photoreaction enhancement [14–17].

Plasmonic hot electrons in noble metals, which was first regarded as a kind of defect [18], have been used to boost many photoreactions including photocatalysis reactions [19,20] and photodetector [21–23]. The existence of hot electrons in superblack CAs has also been confirmed indirectly and leads to ∼8 times enhancement of photocatalytic efficiency [24]. Since the hot electrons in the CAs can strongly couple with other materials, we speculate that they will also be active inside the material. We used to predict that the electrical properties of CAs may change as follows under the illumination: a) electrons are excited into the conduction band, leading to the increase of the carrier concentration and accompanying electrical conductivity; b) after the electron-phonon thermal equilibrium, the scattering of photon to electrons is enhanced and thus reduces the electrons mobility and according conductivity; c) some electrons in oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CAs may transit into conduction band through thermal excitation, leading to the increase of the conductivity. Due to the existence of active hot electrons, even the electromagnetic wave with longer wavelength may interact with CAs, resulting in the electrical response to a wider spectrum.

Since the absorption of light mainly occurs on the surface of superblack materials, the material needs to be thin enough to magnify the interaction of electrons and photons on the surface and reduce the bulk effect. The macro morphology of CA fabricated from the traditional methods of Pekala [25] is usually block or powder, but rarely thin film. Herein, inspired by the preparation method of Leventis [26], we realized the preparation of carbonized resorcinol–formaldehyde (RF) aerogel film with the thickness of hundreds of nanometers by using acetonitrile as solvent, and reducing the concentration of R and F and catalyst content significantly. We found that the conductivity of CA films was significantly increased under laser irradiation, and further studies demonstrated that the conductivity change was caused by the negative temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR) of CA films. Different from the previous studies on amorphous carbon-polymer composites or ordered carbon materials (graphene and carbon nanotube) [27–30], this study finds the intrinsic negative TCR of amorphous carbon aerogel. The superblack CA films exhibit remarkable response in ultrabroad electromagnetic spectrum, even sensitive to 3 cm microwave. This response spectrum is wider than most of the ultra-broadband photodetectors [31–33]. Thus this study may provide a novel route to low-cost and high-efficiency long wave detection.

CA film was prepared by polymerization of resorcinol and formaldehyde, dip-coating, and subsequent carbonization processes. The general preparation process is as follow: 3.236 g resorcinol was dissolved in 116 mL acetonitrile and stirred magnetically for 10 min, and then 4.470 ml formaldehyde was added in drops. After stirred for another 30 min, 0.295 ml dilute hydrochloric acid (0.5 mol/L) was added and stirred continuously for 30 min. The solution was put in an oven at 30 °C for 2 h and then took out for dip-coating. Pre-cleaned quartz glass sheet was immersed in the sol solution for 30 s and then lifted with a constant withdrawal rate to obtain RF aerogel film. After sealed and aged for 14 days, it was carbonized in the tubular furnace for 30 min at 1000 °C under the protection of nitrogen atmosphere to get the final CA films. As shown in Fig. S1, the traditional base-catalysis RF sol was also used for film coating for comparison. Limited by the long gelation time, it is difficult to find a viscosity of the sol suitable for film fabrication. Besides, it takes long time for solvent evaporation. Neither the dip-coating nor the spin-coating can’t result in applicable and smooth RF films. Compared with the original fabrication methods of acid-catalysis RF aerogel [26], the content of HCL was decreased (the ratio of resorcinol to catalyst is 200 compared to 8.4) to better control the sol-gel process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical properties measurement of CA films
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature-resistance relationship and the Hall Effect were measured on the physical property measurement system (Quantum Design PPMS 9, USA). The connecting way was showed in Fig. S2, temperature ranging from 273 K to 373 K and magnetic field from -2-2 T.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectric response test of CA films
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained CA film was scraped into a square with sizes of about 1 cm × 1 cm and with silver colloid coated on two opposite sides to connect the multimeter through copper wire. The resistance and the temperature changes of the CA film were measured under normal irradiation of electromagnetic wave. The distance between light source and CA film is about 10 cm. Temperature change was measured by using the thermal imager (Fotric 225s, USA) and the emissivity of CA film in the thermal image is 0.95.

The morphology of the CA film and RF film was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Philips-XL30FEG, USA) and transmittance electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin, USA). The optical reflectivity was measured from 400 nm to 2000 nm using an UV–vis–NIR spectrometer (V-570, Japan) with an integrating sphere. Raman spectrum (Jobin-Yvon HR800) were used to record the Raman signal from 1000 to 2000 cm−1 using a 514 nm argon ion laser (source power 17 mW). X ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer using an Al Kα source. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Tensor 27, Germany) of the samples were obtained in a KBr tablet.

Fig. 1 shows the preparation process of the CA film and the microstructure of the film. The RF film is obtained by sol-gel polymerization reaction of resorcinol and formaldehyde catalyzed by hydrochloric acid, as shown in Fig. 1a and Movie 1(supporting information). The CA film is obtained through subsequent aging and high temperature carbonization of RF film. It can be seen from the SEM and TEM images that both RF film (Fig. S3) and CA film (Fig. 1b and c) show a network structure formed by the cross-linking and accumulation of particles with diameters of about 10–20 nm, which is similar to the morphology of the acid-catalyzed RF aerogel bulks reported earlier [26]. Compared with aerogel bulks, the network structure of the RF film is denser. This is mainly due to the differences in drying methods between them. CA bulks are fabricated by using CO2 supercritical fluid drying technology, so most pore structures are retained due to the absence of capillary force. In order to simplify the preparation process, ambient drying is adopted for film. According to the cross-profile SEM (inset in Fig. 1b), the thickness of the CA film is about 400 nm, meeting the requirements for subsequent measurements. The thickness can be changed by adjusting the lifting velocity and the viscosity of the sol used for coating.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.01.101

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Movie 1 Preparation of CA films
1
Movie 1 Preparation of CA films

The compositions and structures of the CA films were studied by various characterization methods. According to the XPS spectra (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S4) and the TEM elemental mapping (Fig. 2c), although carbonized at high temperature, some oxygen-containing functional groups still exist on the surface of the CA film. The oxygen content of the film was 10.25% and 6.11% after carbonization at 800 and 1000 °C, respectively. The O 1s spectra shows that there are two peaks centered at 533.7 and 532.3 eV, representing C–O (epoxide and hydroxyl) and CO (carbonyl) respectively. With higher thermal treatment temperature, the relative intensity of the peak representing CO is decreasing. FTIR spectrum also confirmed the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups (Fig. S5). On the other hand, after thermal treatment, some crystalline regions of CA films appeared, which can be verified from high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2d) and the G band in Raman spectra (Fig. 2b).

Fig. 3 a shows the diffuse reflection spectra of the CA films measured by integrating sphere from visible light to near infrared band (300–2000 nm). It can be seen that the reflectance of the CA films is higher than that of the bulk aerogel (0.19% minimum), but the reflectance in the spectrum of 400–2000 nm is below 5%. The reflectance in near infrared are even below 1%, indicating the strong absorption of long wave. At first, the photoelectric response of the CA films was tested with three monochromatic lasers of different wavelengths (532, 635, and 980 nm). We measured the resistance and temperature changes of the CA films carbonized at two different temperature (800 °C and 1000 °C) under the laser irradiation. As shown in Fig. 3b and c, the resistance of the CA films decreases almost linearly with the increase of light power under laser irradiation; while at the same time, the temperature of the film also increases with the optical power under laser irradiation. Under laser irradiation of different wavelengths, ΔR/R0 as a function of temperature are basically the same, which can be confirmed from Fig. 3d. It should be noted that the temperature changes of the CA films recorded is the temperature of the hottest point which cannot represent the precise temperature of the whole films. Thus the resistance change is not strictly corresponding with the temperature change. This behavior, seemingly similar to the photoelectric response is only related to temperature intrinsically. The 532 nm laser (green) has a stronger response than the 980 nm laser and 635 nm laser from Fig. 3b and c, which may be due to the difference in optical power density caused by the different types of lasers used in our experiment. The ΔR/R0–power relationship of pure graphite sheet with a thickness of 0.5 mm was also shown in Fig. S6 for further comparison. It can be seen that there is no similar response, which indicates that the microstructure and component of CA film are not the same as that of pure graphite, and naturally the conductive mechanisms of them are also different.

To further clarify the source of the electrical response, we draw the ΔR/R0–time curve and the temperature–time curve under laser irradiation, Fig. 4 a shows the ΔR/R0–time response curve of the CA film with a carbonization temperature of 800 °C under the irradiation of 532 nm laser through three ON–OFF cycles. The photoelectric response of the film has a good stability and repeatability. As can be seen from the zoomed-in part of the curve, under the illumination, the resistance first decreases rapidly, then slowly, and finally reaches a stable value. This phenomenon has a good correspondence with the infrared thermal image: we can see from the thermal image (Fig. 4c) that at first, the temperature of only one point (where the laser spot at) increases, then the area of the “hot spot” spreads rapidly and the resistance drops at the same time. When the whole CA film is uniformly heated, the rate of resistance change gradually decreases and finally reaches a stabilization. Furthermore, the curve is fitted by an exponential attenuation function with two relaxation times:
R =
R’
0
+
Ae
− t /
τ
1
+
Be
− t /
τ
2
. The fitted data show that the time constants τ1 and τ2 are 13.2 s and 85.4 s, respectively. According to temperature–time curve of the laser spot (Sp 1) and the edge spot (Sp 2) in Fig. 4b, the two time constants probably represent the time during which the temperatures of the laser spot (τ1) and the whole film (τ2) reach an equilibrium, respectively. That is, during laser irradiation, the temperature at the irradiation point first increases rapidly within ∼13 s, while the resistance drops accordingly; then the temperature at the “hot point” is transferred to the whole film region by thermal conduction and reaches the thermal equilibrium at around 85 s, and the value of ΔR/R0 also tends to be stable. In addition, the two-dimensional temperature distribution diagram of CA film is also shown in Fig. S7. The only hot spot at the beginning of laser irradiation and the temperature diffusion of the hot spot causing planar uplift after 180 s irradiation. These further proves the dependence of the photoelectric response on temperature. It is not dominantly induced by the direct photo-generated charge carriers, but the electron-phonon interaction of CA films, which thereby increases the overall temperature. Similar situation can be found in the studies of photoelectric response of carbon nanotubes (CNTs): researchers show through a series of experiments that the photoelectric response of CNTs is not due to the direct generation of photo-induced electrons and holes, but the indirect generation of exciton caused by temperature rising through electron-photo interaction, resulting in the photoelectric response [34].

The decrease of resistance with temperature rising means a negative temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR). Fig. 5 a shows that the resistivity of the CA film decreases linearly with the increase of temperature. According to the formula of TCR:
α =
1
R
T
d
R
T
d T
, it can be calculated that the value of ɑ is about −0.13%/K (1000 °C) to −0.15%/K (800 °C). The resistivity-temperature curve of the CA film after 800 °C carbonization is also shown in Fig. S8. In order to further explore the conductive mechanism of the CA film, Hall Effect tests were performed with common four-probe method under a few temperature points from 273 to 333 K (symmetrization was carried out for the original data to reduce the error). According to the results of Hall Effect test shown in Fig. 5b and c, although the carrier mobility decreases slightly with the rise of temperature, the carrier concentration increases obviously. The behavior is similar to that of semiconductors. This is not in contrast with the inherent impression of carbon material (usually graphite), which is a kind of semi-metal with an electron concentration of ∼1018 cm−3 [35]. It can be calculated that the order of magnitude of the carrier concentration of the CA film is about 1020 cm−3, which is far higher than that of the carrier concentration of ordinary semiconductors. We believe that due to its high carrier concentration, the band structure and carrier distribution of the CA film are likely to be similar to the behavior of semi-metal or degenerate semiconductor. Reviewing the three possible mechanism of electrical conductivity changes presented previously: for a), there ought to be an ultrafast increase of carrier concentration which however has not been observed (Fig. S9). This may be due to the overlap of energy bands [36], which leads to the easy recombination of the electron-hole pairs. This will make the carrier lifetime short and unable to make an effective contribution to the conductivity; for b), the increase of temperature enhances the scattering of phonons on electrons and reduces the electron mobility, which is confirmed by experiments; c) electrons in organic oxygen-containing functional groups with narrow energy band located on the surface take part in electric conduction under thermal excitation, which increases the carrier concentration. According to experimental results, the influence of c) exceeds that of b), resulting in a negative TCR.

Under most conditions, the TCR of a conductor is positive because the electrons mobility is affected by the photon scattering [37]. While in recent years, the negative TCR of some ordered carbon materials such as graphene (including GO and RGO) and CNT (including multi-wall and single-wall) has been found, which usually owns to the increased concentration of thermogenic electron-hole pairs or electric field-promoted tunneling [38–41]. While amorphous carbon is usually dispersed in polymer materials, which increases the possibility of electron hopping and little was investigated about their intrinsic electrical properties [30]. Considering the composition of the CA films, some organic groups are still retained after carbonization, and there are also some graphite regions. CA can be regarded as a complex of graphite microcrystals and carbon quantum dots encased by organic groups. Electrons transfer between their energy level and carbon quantum dots wrapped by organic groups can be regarded as potential electron donators. As temperature increases, the electrons transit from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of carbon dots to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and produce free electrons [42]. The exited free electrons are subsequently injected into the conduction band of graphite, thus increasing the carrier concentration and the Fermi energy level. The HOMO-LUMO gap of carbon dot is dependent on the size of sp2 core and a larger size will cause a narrower gap [43]. The fairly weak peak in emission spectrum of CA films under 230 nm exciting light also confirms the existence of the carbon quantum dots (Fig. S10). It is obvious that after thermal treatment of 800–1000 °C, the sp2 core is large enough. Since the required energy is low, the increase of carrier concentration and the rise of Fermi level are quasi-continuous, just like the continuous injection of water into a pond and the rise of the horizontal plane. The Scheme of the energy bands of CA film is shown in Fig. 5d and the dynamic process of electron transition is shown in Movie 2 (supporting information). The sample with lower carbonization temperature has more oxygen-containing functional groups. There are more extra electrons taking part in the transfer process, making the normalized change in resistance larger (Fig. 3b and c).
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.01.101

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Movie 2 electron transit mechanism

According to Drude model, the absorption of ordinary conductors is mainly due to ohmic loss (joule heat) caused by the electron-phonon interaction. The equivalent current of a single electron induced by long wavelength (low frequency) electromagnetic waves is small. The weakening of hot electron effect, on the other hand, are much smaller with wavelength increasing, and therefore may respond to electromagnetic waves of a broader range. From incident electromagnetic waves to superblack CA, it takes three processes to achieve a state of thermal equilibrium:1) the incident photons interact with the surface electrons, and the photon energy is transferred to the surface electron internal energy, making the surface electrons hot; 2) Surface electrons and internal electrons reach thermal equilibrium through electron-electron interaction; 3) The electrons interact with phonon and make the whole material reaching the final thermal equilibrium. In process 1), due to the small-size-induced higher equivalent electron number density, there is a higher probability of the interaction between surface electrons and the incident photons, causing a higher internal energy (Ese) of the surface electrons. According to:
(1)
T =
2
3 k
E
where T is the temperature, k is the Boltzman constant and E is the internal energy. The temperature rise of surface electrons (Tse) is higher. On the other hand, considering the near-free electron model, the highest occupied state of the electron is Fermi energy E
F when the temperature is close to 0 K:
(2)
E
F
=
h
2
2
m
*
(
3
8 π
n
0
)
2
3
where h is Planck constant, m* is the effective mass of the electron near E
F , and n
0 is the electron concentration. And the expression for the electron heat capacity C
v is:
(3)
C
v
=
n
0
π
2
2
(
k T
E
F
)
k

It can be found that the heat capacity of electrons is directly proportional to
n
0
1
3
. For ultra-black CAs, due to the existence of small-size effect, its equivalent electron number density is greatly increased, resulting in the increase of the heat capacity of electrons C
v . Based on the formula below:
(4)
Q =
C
V
V
Δ T
where Q is the quantity of the heat, C
V is the volumetric heat capacity, V is the volume and ΔT is the temperature difference. The heat absorption (Q
se ) of the surface electrons will increase significantly due to the small size effect. While for the whole materials, its heat capacity is mainly dependent on the phonon. The heat capacity of the electron doesn’t contribute much to the overall heat capacity (C
V ), so the heat capacity doesn’t change significantly. Therefore, due to the increase of heat absorption by surface electrons, the temperature change of the whole material is more obvious than that of the material without small size effect. In another words, surface hot electrons enable CA a higher photo-thermal conversion efficiency [14]. While hot electrons transfer in plasmons occurs at the heterojunctions, there is currently no direct evidence to show that whether the hot electrons take part in the transfer process. Thus, in this study only hot-electron-induced photothermal conversion effects is discussed.

The CA bulks has been studied to show strong and flat absorption properties in the IR spectrum [44]. The transmittance spectrum from 2.5 to 25 μm confirms that CA films have the similar strong absorption properties (Fig. S11). Besides, in our previous work, it has been found that CA bulks show the double negative properties in the spectrum of about 600 MHz-1 GHz, which indirectly prove that CAs have good absorption properties to long wavelength microwave [45]. Thus the response of the CA film to electromagnetic waves of other spectrum was further tested to demonstrate the above discussion, including simulated sunlight of 300–2500 nm emitted by Xe lamp (Fig. 6 b), far infrared rays centered at about 8200 nm emitted by 80 °C hot water (Fig. 6c) and microwave with wavelength of 3 cm emitted by microwave generator (Fig. 6d, the whole experimental apparatus scheme shown in Fig. S12). The specific test schematic diagram is shown in Movie 3 (supporting information), and the final electrical response is −11.6%, −0.25% and −0.06%, respectively. In addition, CA film is also sensitive to the temperature of fingers (thermal images shown in Fig. S14). When fingers are placed on the back of the CA film, the electrical response eventually stabilizes at about 2.5% under thermal conduction through quartz glass (Fig. 6a). It should be noted that in the experiment of response of hot water, the thermal irradiation results in about three quarters of the resistance change and thermal conduction results in about one quarter of that, shown in Fig. S13.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.01.101

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Movie 3 response to electronmagnetic wave
3
Movie 3 response to electronmagnetic wave

The width of the response spectrum is comparable with most of the ultra-broad spectrum photodetectors (370.6 nm-118 μm as far as we know) [33]. Fig. S15 demonstrates a comparison of the ultra-broadband photo-response of different materials with several kinds of response mechanism. The response mechanisms can be roughly divided into two categories: one is the direct photon induced response and the other goes through the indirect photothermal conversion. As is known, the direct photo responses mainly include photoconductive (photogating is also included) and photovoltaic. The ultra-broad band response is achieved by using narrow bandgap materials, focused on topological insulators (TIs such as SnTe and Bi2Te3) and transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) [33,46–48]. Besides, the introduction of defect states is another common method to narrow the gap, while those methods need precise and expensive fabrication process [49–52]. By contrast, thermal induced responses don’t require a narrower but non-overlapping bandgap and have a wider detective spectrum in general [53,54]. In this work, the thermal effect is enhanced by the hot-electron effect, which greatly increase the photo-thermal conversion, making it possible to response to longer wave. However, the response time is much slower than most of the photodetectors mainly due to the high thermal capacity of quartz glass substrate. Besides, the responsivity is also low. This is mainly caused by the only one single component (without heterojunctions) and the simple structure of the device. However, it is the first study on the intrinsic TCR of amorphous carbon materials. It may promote the fundamental understanding of the electronic structure of carbon materials. Also, through refining the microstructure, combining with other materials and improving the structure of devices, CAs are expected to show better performance in the fields of long wave detection and efficient hot electron coupled light-induced reactions.

In conclusion, we predicted and demonstrated the electrical response behavior to a wide spectrum electromagnetic wave (from 300 to 400 nm UV to 3 cm microwave) based on the study of the hot-electron effect in superblack CA. The CA film with a simple device structure and cheap fabrication method shows a responsivity to vis-near infrared light of 0.019–0.022%/mW and to microwave of 0.06%. Furthermore, we found the intrinsic negative TCR (0.13–0.15%/K) of amorphous CA films with a carrier concentration of about 1020, which could be explained by a simplified energy band model of a combination of carbon quantum dots and graphite. With temperature rising, the electrons was first exited in the energy band of carbon quantum dots and then injected into the conduction band of graphite, thus increasing the carrier concentration. With the existence of hot electron effect, the photo-thermal conversion and the consequent response to electromagnetic wave was greatly broadened. This work demonstrates that the broadband photo-response can be achieved by combining properties of high photo-thermal conversion efficiency and TCR of the materials. Besides, the hot-electron effect in small sized CAs is expected to enhance more light-induced reaction including photocatalysis, photovoltaic device and photothermal conversion.

Xiujie Ji: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis. Hongqiang Wang: Writing - review & editing, Formal analysis. Tianze Chen: Investigation. Ting Zhang: Visualization. Junjun Chu: Investigation. Ai Du: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11874284) and the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0204600). And the authors would like to thank Ms. XueSi Hong, Ms. Chenwei Huang, Mr. Haoran Zeng, Mr. Shuo Yuan and Mr. Xudong Cai for their contributions to some of the beautiful figures and movies in this paper.

Multimedia component 4 Multimedia component 4

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.01.101.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An optical dustbin made by the subwavelength-induced super-black carbon aerogels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.Q. Wang, A. Du, Z.H. Zhang, B. Zhou, J. Shen, An optical dustbin made by the subwavelength-induced super-black carbon aerogels, Journal of Materials Research 32(18) (2017) 3524-3531.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Perfect blackbody sheets from nano-precision microtextured elastomers for light and thermal radiation management

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Amemiya, H. Koshikawa, M. Imbe, T. Yamaki, H. Shitomi, Perfect blackbody sheets from nano-precision microtextured elastomers for light and thermal radiation management, Journal of Materials Chemistry C 7(18) (2019) 5418-5425.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cast-and-use super black Coating based on polymer-derived hierarchical porous carbon spheres

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Guo, D. Li, H. Zhao, W. Zou, Z. Yang, Z. Qian, et al., Cast-and-use super black Coating based on polymer-derived hierarchical porous carbon spheres, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces 11(17) (2019) 15945-15951.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yang, Y. Zhang, X. Yang, M. Zhong, Z. Yi, X. Liu, et al., Enhanced photothermal effect in ultralow-density carbon aerogels with microporous structures for facile optical ignition applications, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces 11(7) (2019) 7250-7260.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.K. Hedayati, M. Javaherirahim, B. Mozooni, R. Abdelaziz, A. Tavassolizadeh, V.S.K. Chakravadhanula, et al., Design of a perfect black absorber at visible fequencies using plasmonic metamaterials, Advanced Materials 23(45) (2011) 5410-5414.

Sun, et al., Ultralow percolation threshold and enhanced electromagnetic interference shielding in poly(l-lactide)/multi-walled carbon nanotube nanocomposites with electrically conductive segregated networks, Journal of Materials Chemistry C 5(36) (2017) 9359-9369.
